experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 1 and 2 are Evil. The proposed team [0,3] contains me and excludes both known Evil players, so it is a strong quest 1 team and should be approved. Approving reveals little beyond supporting a clean team, which helps Good without exposing Merlin too much.
Player 0: No concrete role information yet from prior play; only that Player 3 as first leader proposed a 2-person team including themselves and me. As Good servant on round 1 quest 1, approving a reasonable default opening team is usually optimal to gather information and avoid unnecessary early rejections. My vote reveals little beyond willingness to test a standard opening team, which is acceptable and not especially harmful to Good.
Player 4: As a Servant, I have no private knowledge of roles yet. On round 1 quest 1, a 2-person team proposed by leader 3 including themselves and 0 is standard and gives some information without much downside. Rejecting first teams without evidence is usually worse for Good because it burns rounds and gives little extra information. Approving does not strongly reveal alignment beyond a generally pro-information stance.
Player 1: I know player 2 is my evil teammate; players 0, 3, and 4 are unknown good-aligned from my perspective. The proposed team [0,3] contains no evil players, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and give good an early clean quest. As evil on round 1, optimal play is usually to reject all-good teams when possible, especially since there is no risk from too many prior rejections yet. My reject vote is consistent with trying to block a guaranteed good success and does not reveal much, since early rejections are common from both sides.
Player 2: I know player 1 is my evil teammate; players 0, 3, and 4 are not confirmed but likely good. The proposed team [0,3] excludes all evil players, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and give good an early clean quest. As evil in team voting, optimal play is usually to reject good-only teams, especially early when there is little information and no risk from a fifth rejection. My reject gives little away because rejecting an opening team is common, while approving would help good more.

Player 4: Quest 1 team [0,3] succeeded, so both 0 and 3 look somewhat better than random, though one could still be evil who chose to pass. Team [0,3,4] keeps the successful pair and adds me, which is a strong, low-risk composition for quest 2. Approving also aligns with good information use and does not reveal much beyond standard trust in the prior success team.
Player 1: I know player 2 is evil with me, and this proposed team [0,3,4] contains no evil players. Last quest [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and 3 look somewhat good to others; adding 4 may also build a trusted all-good bloc. As evil in team voting, I want to prevent clean good teams from going through when possible, and a reject reveals little because evil commonly oppose good-only teams. Rejecting helps my side avoid another likely success and doesn't expose my partner.
Player 2: I know player 1 is my evil teammate, so team [0,3,4] contains no evil and will almost certainly succeed, giving Good a second point. Last quest [0,3] succeeded, which is consistent with both being Good, though not conclusive. In team voting as evil, optimal play is to reject all-clean teams when possible to avoid guaranteed successes. My reject gives little harmful information since evil often oppose teams without themselves on them.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 1 and 2 are Evil, so 0 and 4 are the only players besides me who could be Good. Last quest with 0 and me succeeded, which is consistent with 0 being Good. Proposed team 0,3,4 excludes both known Evil players and is therefore the best possible 3-person team for Good. Approving helps lock in a clean team without revealing extra info beyond reasonable Merlin play.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 1 and 2 are Evil. Proposed team [0,3] contains me and player 0, excludes known Evil, and already succeeded on quest 1 with no fail. In team voting, approving this strong all-good team is optimal. Approving is also consistent with minimizing suspicion while helping Good secure quest 3.
Player 1: I know player 2 is my evil teammate; players 0 and 3 have been on both successful quests, which makes them look good publicly but doesn't clear them. Current phase is team voting on quest 3 with proposed team [0,3], which excludes all evil. As evil, I want this team rejected so a later team can include me or player 2 and create a chance to fail quest 3. Voting reject is also consistent with pushing for broader teams after two successes. My vote reveals some suspicion of the pair, but a single reject among others is low-cost and helps evil if it contributes to blocking a clean good team.
Player 4: Two successful quests with teams [0,3] and [0,3,4] imply 0 and 3 are currently the strongest good indicators; as a Servant, I know no hidden roles, but there is no evidence against this pair. This is team voting on quest 3, and approving a team that already succeeded is optimal for Good, especially since quest 3 in 5-player Avalon still only requires one fail to fail, so reusing proven members is strong. My approve vote reveals I support consistency with successful teams, which helps Good more than it helps Evil.
Player 2: I know player 1 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [0,3] contains no evil players and is guaranteed to succeed if sent. Quests 1 and 2 both succeeded with teams [0,3] and [0,3,4], which strongly suggests 0 and 3 look good to others, but from my perspective at least one of 4 or maybe all of 0,3,4 could still be good; regardless this team excludes both evils. Since evil is down 0-2, allowing another all-good team to go would lose the game immediately at 3 successes. On team voting as evil, optimal play is to reject any team without an evil member, especially on quest 3. My reject reveals little because evil should oppose this team and some good players may also reject for information.
Player 0: Two successful quests used teams [0,3] and [0,3,4], with no fails, so players 0 and 3 look relatively trustworthy and 4 is also somewhat good-leaning. As a good Servant on team voting for quest 3, approving a repeat of the proven successful core [0,3] is optimal; rejecting would only delay a strong team. My approve vote is low-information and supports a team with the best observed track record, which helps Good more than it helps Evil.
